danger_signals.meaning: Player has lost control; game over state imminent

danger_signals.visual_form: Ball physics moving toward table edges/gutters
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate loss of ball/life if not intercepted by flippers

safety_signals.visual_form: Flipper sprites moving upward at table bottom
safety_signals.meaning: Player has active control; ball can be redirected
Safe scoring zones; interactive elements ready

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ball in motion on table; playfield elements animated and responsive
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Bumpers activate on ball contact; ramps trigger sequences; scoring multipliers active

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Thematic artwork and sprites define each table's world (Android/sci-fi, Jungle/organic, Deep Sea/aquatic, Medieval/Excalibur, etc.)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Scoring mechanics and ramp sequences align with table narrative (e.g., Jungle Pinball tribute to Jill of the Jungle; Space Journey sci-fi aesthetics)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Score counter updates; multiplier indicators visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Consecutive successful hits increase multipliers; ramp sequences unlock progressive bonuses

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ball disappears; visual feedback or animation for drain
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ball count decrements; player returns to plunger or game over if no balls remain

environmental_storytelling.example: Android table communicates sci-fi setting through sprite artwork and mechanical elements; Jungle Pinball evokes organic, dangerous environment through tropical-themed artwork
Table names (Excalibur, Deep Sea, Space Journey, Toy Factory) establish narrative context without explicit story; visual design reinforces theme
Jungle Pinball designed as tribute to Epic's earlier game Jill of the Jungle, using familiar aesthetic to bridge game worlds

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Responds to gravity, flipper contact, bumper collision; physics-driven trajectory
Rotates upward to intercept/launch ball; timing-dependent impact on trajectory
Activates on collision; launches ball in opposing direction; visual feedback (bright sprite flash)
Directs ball along predefined path; may trigger sequence bonuses or lane locks
Captures ball; ends current ball life; triggers game-over if no balls remain

notes: Epic Pinball operates in a pure mechanical paradigm with minimal narrative world-state complexity. Communication is primarily through table-specific theming (visual aesthetic design) and physics-based feedback. No faction systems, political situations, or dynamic world-state changes exist. Danger/safety signals derive entirely from pinball mechanics (tilt, drain, flipper control). Manifestation is deterministic and physics-driven rather than narrative-driven. The 1993 technology (320x240, raster graphics) constrains visual communication to sprite-based thematic elements and fundamental HUD feedback (score, ball count, lives).
